summary.brief: Calloway v. McKenna. Medical malpractice case involving cardiology.

evidence_index.text: James Calloway met with Dr. Brarens, a podiatrist, to discuss pain that he was having in his foot and ankle.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: James met with Dr. Peter McKenna, a plastic surgeon at CIPS, to discuss having a liposuction procedure while he was off of work for the ankle surgery.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. McKenna evaluated James for his risk of deep vein thrombosis (“DVT”) and pulmonary embolism (“PE”) by using a self-evaluation worksheet.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: James had ankle surgery and was instructed not to put any weight on that leg for eight weeks.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On June 20, 2019, James had a successful liposuction surgery.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: James began to experience congestion. He went to his primary care physician’s office on July 11 and met with the nurse practitioner, who instructed him to go to the emergency room immediately.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: James eventually went to Bethesda North Hospital and was informed that he had developed blood clots and needed surgery.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: James passed away on July 14. Following his death, the coroner’s office determined his cause of death as complications from a DVT/PE blood clot.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The jury found that Dr. McKenna was negligent by not adequately explaining to James his correct risk level and that he failed to obtain informed consent.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The jury also found that the lack of informed consent was not the proximate cause of James’s death.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The jury’s verdict in favor of Dr. McKenna and CIPS was not against the manifest weight of the evidence.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: We conclude that no exceptional circumstance exists, and we decline to label as plainly erroneous the conclusion section of the jury instructions.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: James Calloway met with Dr. Brarens, a podiatrist, to discuss pain in his foot and ankle. He decided to have foot surgery scheduled for May 30, 2019. On May 29, 2019, he met with Dr. Peter McKenna to discuss having a liposuction procedure while he was off work for the ankle surgery.
initial_state.initial_working_problem: Foot and ankle pain requiring surgery

requestables.name: Podiatry Consultation
requestables.description: Consultation with Dr. Brarens regarding foot and ankle pain

reveal.result_summary: James decided to have foot surgery scheduled for May 30, 2019.
reveal.decision_impact: Led to scheduling of ankle surgery

requestables.name: Plastic Surgery Consultation
requestables.description: Consultation with Dr. McKenna regarding liposuction

reveal.result_summary: James discussed having a liposuction procedure while off work for ankle surgery.
reveal.clinical_significance: Consideration of additional procedure during recovery

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: James presents with foot and ankle pain and considers additional procedures.

decision_points.prompt: What should be done to assess DVT risk?

options.description: Conduct a comprehensive DVT risk assessment including all relevant factors
options.clinical_reasoning: Comprehensive assessment is necessary to accurately determine risk

options.description: Use a self-evaluation worksheet without considering all factors
options.clinical_reasoning: Incomplete assessment may miss critical risk factors

actual_action_defendant.description: Dr. McKenna used a self-evaluation worksheet and did not include the fact that James would be in a plaster cast.
actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Dr. McKenna concluded James had a moderate risk for DVT.

expected_action_court.description: A comprehensive DVT risk assessment should have been conducted.
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence established the need for a thorough risk assessment.

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Based on evidence E009, the assessment was incomplete.
explanation.what_should_have_happened: A comprehensive risk assessment should have been conducted.

patient_outcome.description: James developed blood clots and passed away due to complications from DVT/PE.

key_findings: Dr. McKenna was found negligent in not adequately explaining DVT risk.
Lack of informed consent was not the proximate cause of death.

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to adequately assess and communicate DVT risk
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Comprehensive risk assessment might have altered management

factual_timeline.event: James met with Dr. Brarens for foot and ankle pain
Consultation with Dr. McKenna for liposuction
James passed away due to DVT/PE complications

tests_performed.result: Moderate risk assessed without considering plaster cast

allegations.allegation: Failure to adequately assess and communicate DVT risk

court_findings.finding: Dr. McKenna was negligent but lack of informed consent was not the proximate cause of death
